frameworks.name: neuromodulatory gain control
frameworks.one_line: prior view that catecholamines and acetylcholine share similar single-neuron gain effects

frameworks.name: large-scale cortical interaction analysis
frameworks.one_line: dissociates neuromodulator effects at the level of cortex-wide between-area interactions

frameworks.name: excitation-inhibition balance circuit model
frameworks.one_line: circuit model linking E/I balance and intracortical transmission to interaction changes

frameworks.name: decision-making noise model
frameworks.one_line: links catecholaminergic E/I shift to increased choice variability

core_question: Do catecholamines and acetylcholine play distinct roles in shaping cortex-wide interactions and behavioral variability despite similar single-neuron gain effects?
key_assumptions: single-neuron gain effects do not fully capture neuromodulator function
cortex-wide interactions reflect circuit properties beyond local response gain
circuit modeling can map population-level changes to E/I and transmission parameters
key_findings: catecholamine boost increased cortex-wide interactions during task but not rest
acetylcholine boost decreased cortex-wide interactions during rest but not task
circuit model attributes dissociation to differential E/I balance vs intracortical transmission changes
catecholaminergic mechanism predicted and produced noisier perceptual and value-based decisions
interpretations: catecholamines and acetylcholine have a double dissociation in modulating cortical interactions
altered E/I balance under catecholamines causally links to behavioral variability
